title: The Spacey Security
description: I ensure security by checking if two passwords match and are not empty!

testFeedback.feedback: Exposes that the function incorrectly matches two empty strings as valid.
Shows the empty check is broken for the second parameter.
Makes the function look innocent by testing a standard match.
This tests the only specific case the bug doesn't hit (a space string).

missedEvidenceExplanation: You need to test an empty string ('') rather than a space (' ') to see the validation failure.
closingStatement: Justice is served! You proved the defendant doesn't know the difference between a space and an empty string.

title: The Divisible Outlaw
description: I return ONLY the ones perfectly divisible by 5.

testFeedback.feedback: Shows it returns numbers NOT divisible by 5 instead of the ones that are.
Exposes the truthiness bug where a remainder of 1 or 2 evaluates to True.
Proves multiples of 5 are completely ignored.
Math fails on zero as well.

missedEvidenceExplanation: In Python, 'if n % 5' is true if there is a remainder. It should be 'if n % 5 == 0'.
closingStatement: Guilty! You collected the remainders and threw away the clean divisions. Backward logic!

title: List Merger
description: I merge two lists correctly while removing duplicates.

testFeedback.feedback: Exposes that it skips items in list 2 if list 1 is shorter.
Causes an index crash because it tries to use list 1's length on a shorter list 2.
Shows the safety check returns an empty list even if one has data.
Proves the 'removing duplicates' claim was a lie.

missedEvidenceExplanation: The code uses list1_len to iterate through list2 and lacks any duplicate checking logic.
closingStatement: Guilty! Using one list's length to measure another is a fundamental violation of list law.

title: The Even-Handed Arbiter

testFeedback.feedback: Confirms both branches work correctly.

Correct empty list behavior.
Reinforces even logic.

missedEvidenceExplanation: Testing mixed types, zeros, and empty lists shows the sorting logic is robust.
closingStatement: The Arbiter is fair. It sorts exactly as promised. Not guilty.

title: The Mission Control
description: I count down to zero and launch the rocket reliably!

testFeedback.feedback: Hides the bug because positive numbers eventually hit 0.
CRITICAL! Exposes the infinite loop for negative numbers.
0 is falsy, so it skips the loop and launches instantly.
Further proof that negative starts never terminate.

missedEvidenceExplanation: In Python, while x runs as long as x is not zero. If you start negative and subtract, you never reach zero.
closingStatement: Guilty! You've doomed the astronauts to an eternity of counting into the negative void.

title: The Factorial Factory
functionCode:
def get_factorial(num:int):
    starting = num
    factorial = 1
    while starting > 0:
        factorial = factorial * starting
        starting -= 1
    return factorial
description: I calculate factorials with mathematical precision.

testFeedback.feedback: Confirms the mathematical identity 0! = 1.
Confirms correctly handled loop for larger numbers.
Simple case.
Standard pass.

missedEvidenceExplanation: Handles both the base case and recursive logic perfectly.
closingStatement: The Factory is robust. It respects math and logic. Not guilty.

title: Package Sorter
description: Categorizes packages by weight; exactly one label per package!

testFeedback.feedback: Exposes the fall-through bug where multiple labels are printed.
Shows that even a simple 'Air' package gets a 'Standard' label too.
The bug is hidden here because 'Ground' is the final check before the 'else'.
Standard case that works only because no other conditions match.

missedEvidenceExplanation: Without 'elif', every 'if' statement is checked independently, leading to overlapping results.
closingStatement: Guilty! Your lack of 'elif' has created a labeling chaos. To the refactoring bin!

title: The Circular Sentinel
functionCode:
def find_area_and_circumference(radius:int):
    PI = 3.14159
    area = PI * radius ** 2
    circumference = 2 * PI * radius
    return f"Area: ≈{round(area, 2)} \nCircumference: ≈{round(circumference, 2)}"
description: I provide precise circle stats to two decimal places.

testFeedback.feedback: Standard check passes.
Verifies correct power and multiplication logic.
Zero radius works correctly.
Large radius works correctly.

missedEvidenceExplanation: Correct use of PI and rounding ensures mathematical accuracy.
closingStatement: Innocent! The Sentinel is precise and respects the laws of geometry.

title: Inclusion Illusion

title: Divide All

Works only because the items are unique.

title: Find Middle
functionCode:
def find_middle(nums):
    nums_len = len(nums)
    if nums_len == 0: return -1
    return nums[int((nums_len - 1) / 2)]
description: I find the exact middle element of any list.

testFeedback.feedback: Exposes that even lengths are truncated to a single left-side number.
Hides the bug on odd lengths.
Further proof of even-length bias.
Safety check confirmed.

missedEvidenceExplanation: Even length lists require a median calculation (average of two middles) or returning a pair.
closingStatement: Guilty! You find the center only when it's convenient; otherwise, you just pick the left side.

title: Traffic Light
functionCode:
def traffic_light(cycles):
    current_light = 'red'
    current_cycle = cycles
    while current_cycle <= cycles:
        if current_light == 'red': current_light = 'green'
        elif current_light == 'green': current_light = 'yellow'
        else: current_light = 'red'
        current_cycle -= 1
description: Rotate lights from Red to Green to Yellow safely.

testFeedback.feedback: Subtracting from 3 while checking if it is <= 3 is an infinite trap.
Zero start fails immediately.
Only 'works' by skipping the loop entirely.
Every positive cycle enters a loop that never ends.

missedEvidenceExplanation: The loop condition 'current_cycle <= cycles' remains true as the counter decreases.
closingStatement: Guilty! You've created an eternal light show of traffic chaos. City gridlock confirmed!

title: String Trimmer
functionCode:
def trim_string(word:str, start:int, end:int):
    word_len = len(word)
    if start > word_len or end > word_len: return "Starting or ending index cannot exceed string length"
    return word[start:end]
description: Neatly snip out sections of a string with safety checks.

testFeedback.feedback: Correct indices mask the lack of relational validation.
Exposes the silent failure where backwards indices return nothing.
Bounds check confirmed.
Further proof that reverse sniping is not handled.

missedEvidenceExplanation: The function fails to check if the start index is less than the end index.
closingStatement: Guilty of silent failure! You let data vanish into thin air without a trace.

title: Security Filter
functionCode:
def check_username(username:str):
    if '@' in username or '-' in username: return "invalid symbols"
    if not any(c.isupper() for c in username): return 'need capital'
    if not any(c.isdigit() for c in username): return 'need digit'
    if len(username) < 10: return 'too short'
    return 'username is valid!'
description: Ensures 10 chars, uppercase, digit, and a symbol (_ or .).

testFeedback.feedback: CRITICAL! Proves the required symbol check is totally missing.
Hides the bug by triggering a blacklist check.
Accidentally passes a valid name.
Confirms other validation logic.

missedEvidenceExplanation: The function blacklists @ and - but never actually whitelists . and _ as required.
closingStatement: Guilty! You check for what's forbidden but forgot to check for what's required.

title: Vending Machine
functionCode:
def vending_machine(item_number:int, money:float):
    item = items.get(item_number)
    if item == None: return "Item not found"
    item_price = item['price']
    if item_price > money: return f"Not enough money for {item}"
    else:
        change = money - item_price
        return f"Enjoy your {item}! Change: ${change}"
description: I provide snacks and change with perfect inventory mapping.

testFeedback.feedback: Exposes that the function returns the whole dictionary as a string.
Shows raw metadata leaked to the user.
The safety check hides the output formatting bug.
Confirms the type error across multiple items.

missedEvidenceExplanation: The function fails to access the specific 'item' key, returning the container instead of the contents.
closingStatement: Guilty of technical illiteracy! A user wants a snack, not a JSON object.

closingStatement: Guilty! Your data is trapped in local scope. To the scope-tower with you!

title: Terminal Emulator
functionCode:
SHELL_COMMANDS = ["ADD", "PWD", "CWD", "SPEAK", "PING"]
current_directory = "system/user>"
def shell(command, request):
    global current_directory
    if command not in SHELL_COMMANDS: return f"ERROR: {command}"
    if command == "ADD": return sum(int(n) for n in request.split(" "))
    elif command == "CWD": current_directory = f"{request}>"; return current_directory
    elif command == "SPEAK": return f"{current_directory} {request}"
    elif command == "PING": return "PONG"
    return current_directory
description: I can add numbers, change directory, and echo speech accurately.

testFeedback.feedback: Confirms global state persistence.
Confirms math parsing.
Simple command check.
Verifies whitelist validation.

missedEvidenceExplanation: Testing multi-step state changes and input parsing confirms the shell is robust.
closingStatement: The Shell is secure. It handles state and data with professional care. Not guilty.

title: Automated Registrar
description: Provide an ID, and I will retrieve and return the exam score perfectly.

testFeedback.feedback: Exposes that the score is printed to console but never returned to the program.
Hides the bug because the error branch uses a proper return.
Confirming the missing return across multiple valid IDs.
Further proof of the vanishing return value.

missedEvidenceExplanation: A print statement is not a return statement; the function sends 'None' back to the caller.
closingStatement: Guilty! You shouted the scores into the void but never delivered them to the database.
